name: Foundations — Medical English & Communication
_note: For pre-clinical (MS1-2) IMGs before clinical clerkships. Scores LANGUAGE and COMMUNICATION, never clinical reasoning/workup. Metric keys are kept identical to the standard rubric so self-assessment, scores, and DB columns keep working — only the labels and anchors are re-pitched for beginners.

anchors.9-10: Clear, well-formed sentences; natural word order throughout
anchors.7-8: Occasional minor errors; meaning always clear
anchors.5-6: Some errors in tense/articles/word order, but understandable
anchors.3-4: Frequent errors; listener must work to understand
anchors.1-2: Errors make most sentences hard to follow

anchors.9-10: Correct, natural medical English for body parts and symptoms
anchors.5-6: Uses some everyday words where a medical term would help, or small misuses
anchors.3-4: Often reaches for the wrong word or cannot find the term

anchors.9-10: Warm greeting, clear self-introduction, listens and responds to what the patient says
anchors.7-8: Polite and responsive; minor lapses in rapport
anchors.5-6: Gets the basics done but feels mechanical or misses the patient's cues
anchors.3-4: Little warmth; talks over or ignores the patient's responses
anchors.1-2: No rapport; patient is not acknowledged as a person

anchors.9-10: Smooth, steady pace; clearly understandable pronunciation
anchors.7-8: Occasional hesitation; pronunciation rarely impedes understanding
anchors.5-6: Noticeable pauses or pronunciation that needs listener effort
anchors.1-2: Halting delivery or pronunciation that blocks understanding

anchors.9-10: Exceptional empathy and professionalism; creates psychological safety
anchors.7-8: Good empathy; acknowledges emotion; polite and professional
anchors.5-6: Adequate but mechanical empathy; some emotional cues missed
anchors.3-4: Little empathic response; information delivery prioritized
anchors.1-2: No empathic response; dismissive or unprofessional

checklist.item: introduced themselves and their role (e.g. medical student)
confirmed or used the patient's name
opened with an open-ended question (e.g. 'What brings you in today?')
asked the patient to describe the main symptom in their own words
used plain language / explained any medical word used
showed empathy or acknowledged the patient's feelings
checked the patient's understanding or summarized back
asked if the patient had any questions

empathy_markers: used the patient's name at least once
acknowledged the patient's worry or discomfort
used a reassuring or supportive phrase
gave the patient space to speak without interrupting
